revision.comment: Honda Chikaatsu text
revision.text:
'''''Michi no taigen''''' (道之大原) was originally written in March 1883 by [[w:en:Honda Chikaatsu|Honda Chikaatsu]] ([[w:ja:本田親徳|本田親徳]]), a 19th-century Japanese writer, in Literary Chinese (hence the lack of Japanese ''[[w:en:Kana|kana]]'' in the text, although the text also has various Japanese idiosyncrasies). ''Michi no taigen'' (道之大原) can also be found in ''The essence of chinkon kishin'' ([[commons:Category:鎮魂帰神の極意|鎮魂帰神の極意]], ''Chinkon kishin no gokui''), which was published by [[w:en:Shintō Tenkōkyo|Shintō Tenkōkyo]] founder Tomokiyo Yoshisane ([[w:ja:友清歓真|友清歓真]]) in 1920. This version is based on Tomokiyo's 1920 edition. The text consists of 24 sections about the nature of the divine, the formation of the spirit and the soul, and so on. Some of the punctuation has been added from the ''Michi no taigen'' text in: Suzuki, Shigemichi 鈴木重道 (ed.). 1976. ''Honda Chikaatsu zenshū'' 本田親徳全集. Kawaguchi: Sangabō 山雅房.
Some corrections to Tomokiyo's (1920) ''Michi no taigen'' text have been included from: Satō, Akihiko 佐藤卿彦. 1978. ''Kenshin Honda reigaku hōten'' 顕神本田霊学法典. Kawaguchi: Sangabō 山雅房. These corrections are given in square brackets [ ]. ==Original text== ;道之大原註釋 :本田親德先生遺稿 :友清九語謹註 (1) :天[主]創造一物。 :悉因力德。 :故善惡相混 ::美醜互交。 (2) :萬物之中也者。 :有形之中也。 :其中可測矣。 :神界之中也者。 :無形之中也。 :其中不可測矣。 :勿混語。 (3) :顯界之活物者係 ::大國主之所轄。 :然而不能悉守其魂 ::乃魄體之故也。 (4) :漢人所謂中庸、中和、大中。 :其中者與神父之中逈別。 :勿同視。 (5) :人心也者大精神之分派。 :故無生無死。 :爲之所制御。 :而今時以太陽、大地、大陰及人魂。 :爲各位之守神。 :悉皆無神府之說。 :不可信從。 (6) :上帝以四魂一靈造心。 :而賦之活物。 :地主以三元八力造體。 :而與之萬有。 :故守其靈者其體。 :守其體者其靈。 :菲有他神守之也。 :是乃神府之命永遠不易。 (7) :至聖大賢 ::斯民所稱。 :神眼視之 ::未盡全美。 :况於其他乎。 :故先靈不能守後魂必矣。 (8) :大精神之爲體也。 :至大無外 ::至小無內。 :若無所在。 :若無不所在。 :聖眼不能視之。 :賢口不能語之。 :故皇典曰隱身也者 ::則神之義。 :宜哉其靈靈妙妙。 (9) :大精神也者。 :聽於無聲。 :視於無形。 :故知旣往 ::察未然。 :大精神以此至靈賚神子。 :神子尊奉 ::竟成至德。 :是治心之本。 :修身之要。 (10) :神子治善心。 :大精神與之靈魂。 :神子亂良行。 :大精神奪之靈魄。 :其與奪之速如影從形。 :豈可不畏哉大精神。 (11) :大精神無惡 ::而始祖亦無惡。 :裔孫千百中 ::偶有惡罪者 ::悉皆自業自得。 :非始祖之遺。 :神典曰改言乃改過 ::無惡之意。 :何碌碌待死後之爲。 (12) :省。耻。悔。畏。 :覺之五情含有靈魂中。 :乃神明之戒律也。 :末世無識妄作戒律眩惑後學。 :可謂神府之罪奴。 (13) :尊心卑體爲善之本。 :貴體賤心成惡之始。 :故曰善天下所公共。 :惡一人所私有。 :正心德行者善也。 :不正無行者惡也。 :均是神子。 :其相遠恰如雲埿。 :是夫誰過。 (14) :荒魂者神勇。 :和魂者神親。 :奇魂者神智。 :幸魂者神愛。 :乃所謂靈魂 ::而直靈者主宰之。 :俗學不識。 :以荒和爲心之體。 :以奇幸爲心之用。 :不知直靈爲何物。 :豈可不悲哉。 (15) :幽齋以靈對靈。 :顯齋以形對形。 :故幽齋無神像宮社 ::而祈眞神。 :顯齋有神像宮殿 ::而祭衆神。 :俗學蒙眛不知古義。 :混以爲一。 :岐以爲萬。 :無所底止。 :實祭義之大疵。 (16) :所貴于身者。 :名、位、壽、富。 :而與[奪]之者大靈魂也。 :是即神賦之正欲。 :俗學不悟自暴自棄。 :將求貴于外。 :何夫可得哉。 (17) :人皆以爲審判在死後 ::而賞罰不在生前。 :故輕于生 ::而重于死。 :是則蒙神誅之原。 :繫天獄之因[者]。 :神眼赫赫 ::固無幽顯。 :死生一理。 :何爲二之。 (18) :天降生民有一生 ::而無再生。 :是乃天命。 :然而世間往往有言體甦者。 :悉皆詐僞。 :日照神鏡竟觸神怒體斃魂誅。 :亦何望帝側噫。 (19) :萬物精神亦神之所賦與。 :雖然其所受[之]體 ::有尊、有卑、有大、有小。 :故各有美、有惡、有賢、有愚。 :其千變萬化 ::如無究盡。 :大哉大精神之用。 (20) :靈學者以淨心爲本。 :故皇神以鎭魂爲之主。 :百[姓]尊奉日練眞心。 :令義解云鎭者安也 ::言招離遊之運魂留身體之中府。 :可見其國家之重典。 :今人蒙眛頑乎不顧。 :求法于外。 :尋術于異。 :慣習爲常。 :汚穢日加。 :噫悲[矣]哉。 (21) :古稱體曰命。 :稱靈曰神。 :命也者體異體別也。 :神也者幽體隱身之義也。 :後學鹵莽不達古義。 :命神混用。 :幽顯同稱。 :可謂亂。 (22) :小精神 ::動靜無常 ::出沒無窮。 :顯其聲音。 :形其皮膚。 :聖賢不能秘其意。 :小人不能隱其想。 :乃神憲之不可掩者蓋如斯。 (23) :神光謂之高皇產靈。 :神温謂之神皇產靈。 :皆係于始祖之所名。 (24) :藥、浴、防、棄、避之五術 ::固在衆情中。 :後世動作異行 ::猥誦奇咒。 :欲潔其身 ::反汚其體。 :實白圭之玷。 ==English translation== (Google Gemini 2.5 Pro translation; please check for errors and improvements) '''Annotations on the Great Origin of the Way :''(Dō no Taigen Chūshaku)'' ;Posthumous Manuscripts of Master Honda Chikaatsu ;Respectfully Annotated by Tomokiyo Kyūgo ;(1) :When the Lord of Heaven [天主, ''Tenshu''] creates a single thing, it is done entirely through His power and virtue. :Therefore, good and evil are intermingled, and beauty and ugliness intersect. ;(2) :The "within" of the ten thousand things is a "within" that has form. :Its interior can be measured. :The "within" of the divine realm is a "within" that is formless. :Its interior cannot be measured. :Do not speak of them as if they are the same. ;(3) :The living things of the manifest world [顯界, ''kenkai''] are governed by Ōkuninushi [大國主, a prominent Shinto deity of the terrestrial realm]. :However, he cannot entirely protect their spirit, for it is a matter of the soul-body [魄體, ''hakutai'' - the physical or lower aspect of the soul]. ;(4) :What the Chinese call the "Mean" (''Zhongyong''), "Central Harmony" (''Zhonghe''), or the "Great Center" (''Daizhong'')—this "center" is vastly different from the "Center" of the divine way. :Do not view them as the same. ;(5) :The human heart [人心, ''jinshin''] is a branch of the Great Spirit [大精神, ''Dai-seishin'' - the ultimate, supreme God]. :Therefore, it has no birth and no death. :It is by this that one should be controlled. :However, in the present age, people take the Sun, the Earth, the Moon, and human souls to be their respective guardian deities. :These are all teachings that lack the doctrine of the Divine Office [神府, ''Shinfu'' - the true divine system]. :They should not be believed or followed. ;(6) :The Supreme God [上帝, ''Jōtei''] creates the heart from Four Souls and One Spirit [四魂一霊, ''shikon ichirei''] and bestows it upon living things. :The Master of the Earth [地主, ''Jinushi''] creates the body from Three Elements and Eight Powers [三元八力, ''sangen hachiriki''] and gives it to all of existence. :Therefore, that which protects one's spirit is the body, and that which protects one's body is the spirit. :There is no other god that guards them. :This is the command of the Divine Office, eternally unchanging. ;(7) :Even the greatest sages and wisest men acclaimed by the people are not, when seen through the eye of God, entirely perfect. :How much more so for all others? Therefore, it is certain that an ancestral spirit cannot protect a descendant's soul. ;(8) :The body of the Great Spirit is so vast that there is nothing outside it, and so minuscule that there is nothing inside it. :It is as if it exists nowhere, yet it is as if there is nowhere it does not exist. :The eye of a sage cannot perceive it; the mouth of a wise man cannot speak of it. :Therefore, the Imperial Canons [皇典, ''kōten''] state: "To be of hidden body is the definition of a god [神, ''kami'']." How wondrously spiritual and exquisitely mysterious it is! ;(9) :The Great Spirit hears what has no sound and sees what has no form. :Therefore, it knows the past and perceives the not-yet-happened. :The Great Spirit bestows this ultimate spirituality upon the Children of God [神子, ''shinko'' - humanity]. :The Children of God who revere and uphold this will achieve the highest virtue. :This is the foundation of governing the heart and the essential of cultivating the self. ;(10) :When a Child of God cultivates a good heart, the Great Spirit grants them a spiritual soul [靈魂, ''reikon'']. :When a Child of God disorders their good conduct, the Great Spirit seizes their soul-essence [靈魄, ''reihaku'']. :The speed of this granting and seizing follows as a shadow follows a form. :How can one not be in awe of the Great Spirit? ;(11) :The Great Spirit has no evil, and the original ancestor also had no evil. :That among the thousands of descendants there are occasionally those with evil and sin is entirely the result of their own actions and their own reaping. :It is not an inheritance from the original ancestor. :The Divine Canons [神典, ''shinten''] state: "To correct one's words is to correct one's faults," which means to be without evil. :Why do people absent-mindedly wait for what comes after death? ;(12) :Reflection, shame, remorse, awe, and awakening: these five emotions are contained within the soul. :They are the commandments of the divine intelligence. :In these latter days, the ignorant create arbitrary precepts to dazzle and confuse later students. :They can be called the criminal slaves of the Divine Office. ;(13) :To esteem the heart and humble the body is the foundation of goodness. :To value the body and cheapen the heart is the beginning of evil. :Therefore, it is said: "Goodness is the common property of the world; evil is the private possession of a single person." Those with a correct heart and virtuous conduct are good. :Those with an incorrect heart and no virtuous conduct are evil. :Though they are all Children of God, the distance between them is like that between the clouds and the mud. :Whose fault is this? ;(14) :The Wild Soul [荒魂, ''aramitama''] is divine valor. :The Harmonious Soul [和魂, ''nigimitama''] is divine affinity. :The Wondrous Soul [奇魂, ''kushimitama''] is divine wisdom. :The Fortunate Soul [幸魂, ''sachimitama''] is divine love. :These are what is called the spiritual soul, and the Upright Spirit [直靈, ''naohi''] is their master. :Vulgar scholarship does not recognize this, taking the Wild and Harmonious souls as the soul's substance, and the Wondrous and Fortunate souls as its function. :It does not know what the Upright Spirit is. :Is this not to be lamented? ;(15) :In esoteric rites [幽齋, ''yūsai''], one communicates spirit to Spirit. :In exoteric rites [顯齋, ''kensai''], one communicates form to form. :Therefore, esoteric rites have no divine images or shrines, and one prays to the true God. :Exoteric rites have divine images and palaces, and one worships the multitude of gods. :Vulgar scholarship is blind and ignorant of the ancient meaning, mixing them into one, or branching them into ten thousand, with no end to the confusion. :This is truly the great flaw in the meaning of worship. ;(16) :What is valued in one's life are fame, status, longevity, and wealth. :The one who grants and takes these away is the Great Spirit Soul [大靈魂, ''daireikon'']. :This is the proper desire bestowed by God. :Vulgar scholarship, not understanding this, is reckless and self-abandoning, seeking to find value in external things. :How could it possibly be attained? ;(17) :Everyone believes that judgment occurs after death and that reward and punishment do not happen in life. :Therefore, they take life lightly and death gravely. :This is the origin of incurring divine punishment and the cause of being bound in the celestial prison. :The eye of God is brilliant and makes no distinction between the unseen and the manifest worlds. :Life and death are a single principle. :Why make them into two? ;(18) :Heaven gives birth to people with one life and no second life. :This is the Mandate of Heaven. :However, in the world, there are often those who speak of the body's resurrection. :This is all fraud and falsehood. :When illuminated by the divine mirror of the sun, they will eventually incur divine wrath, their bodies will perish and their souls will be punished. :What hope have they then for being by the Emperor's [God's] side? Alas. ;(19) :The spirits of the ten thousand things are also bestowed by God. :However, the bodies they receive have distinctions of noble and humble, large and small. :Therefore, each has beauty and ugliness, wisdom and foolishness. :Their thousand changes and ten thousand transformations are as if without end. :How vast is the working of the Great Spirit! ;(20) :Spiritology [靈學, ''reigaku''] takes the purification of the heart as its foundation. :Therefore, the Imperial Deities [皇神, ''kōshin''] take the pacification of the soul [鎭魂, ''chinkon''] as their main principle. :The people revere and uphold this, daily training their true hearts. :The commentary on the ancient law code [令義解, ''Ryō no gige''] says: "'Pacification' means 'to soothe'; it speaks of summoning the wandering vital soul to remain in the central府 (''fu'', office/seat) of the body." One can see this was a vital rite of the nation. :People today are ignorant and stubbornly disregard it, seeking methods abroad and searching for arts in foreign lands. :This has become a habit, and their defilement increases daily. :Alas, how tragic this is! ;(21) :The ancients called the body ''inochi'' [命, life]. :They called the spirit ''kami'' [神, god/spirit]. :''Inochi'' refers to the distinctness and separateness of bodies. :''Kami'' refers to the meaning of an esoteric body or a hidden form. :Later students are careless and do not grasp the ancient meaning, using ''inochi'' and ''kami'' interchangeably and referring to the unseen and manifest worlds by the same name. :This can be called chaos. ;(22) :The small spirit [小精神, ''shō-seishin'' - the individual human spirit] is in constant motion and stillness, appearing and disappearing without end. :It is revealed in one's voice and takes form on one's skin. :A sage cannot hide his intentions; a lesser person cannot conceal their thoughts. :That the laws of God [神憲, ''shinken''] cannot be obscured is indeed so. ;(23) :The Divine Light is called ''Takamimusubi'' [高皇產靈, a primordial creator deity]. :The Divine Warmth is called ''Kamimusubi'' [神皇產靈, another primordial creator deity]. :Both are tied to the names of the original ancestors. ;(24) :The five arts of medicine, bathing, defending, discarding, and avoiding are certainly within the sentiments of the populace. :In later generations, people created different practices, basely chanting strange spells. :In their desire to purify the self, they instead defile the body. :This is truly a flaw in a perfect white jewel.

'''''Michi no Taigen''''' (道之大原) was originally written in March 1883 by [[w:en:Honda Chikaatsu|Honda Chikaatsu]] ([[w:ja:本田親徳|本田親徳]]), a 19th-century Japanese writer, in Literary Chinese (hence the lack of Japanese ''[[w:en:Kana|kana]]'' in the text, although the text also has various Japanese idiosyncrasies). ''Michi no taigen'' (道之大原) can also be found in ''The essence of chinkon kishin'' ([[commons:Category:鎮魂帰神の極意|鎮魂帰神の極意]], ''Chinkon kishin no gokui''), which was published by [[w:en:Shintō Tenkōkyo|Shintō Tenkōkyo]] founder Tomokiyo Yoshisane ([[w:ja:友清歓真|友清歓真]]) in 1920. This version is based on Tomokiyo's 1920 edition. The text consists of 24 sections about the nature of the divine, the formation of the spirit and the soul, and so on. Some of the punctuation has been added from the ''Michi no taigen'' text in: Suzuki, Shigemichi 鈴木重道 (ed.). 1976. ''Honda Chikaatsu zenshū'' 本田親徳全集. Kawaguchi: Sangabō 山雅房.
Some corrections to Tomokiyo's (1920) ''Michi no taigen'' text have been included from: Satō, Akihiko 佐藤卿彦. 1978. ''Kenshin Honda reigaku hōten'' 顕神本田霊学法典. Kawaguchi: Sangabō 山雅房. :神温謂之神皇產靈。 :皆係于始祖之所名。 (24) :藥、浴、防、棄、避之五術 ::固在衆情中。 :後世動作異行 ::猥誦奇咒。 :欲潔其身 ::反汚其體。 :實白圭之玷。 ==English translation== (Google Gemini 2.5 Pro translation; please check for errors and improvements) '''Annotations on the Great Origin of the Way :''(Dō no Taigen Chūshaku)'' ;Posthumous Manuscripts of Master Honda Chikaatsu ;Respectfully Annotated by Tomokiyo Kyūgo ;(1) :When the Lord of Heaven [天主, ''Tenshu''] creates a single thing, it is done entirely through His power and virtue. :Therefore, good and evil are intermingled, and beauty and ugliness intersect. ;(2) :The "within" of the ten thousand things is a "within" that has form. :Its interior can be measured. :The "within" of the divine realm is a "within" that is formless. :Its interior cannot be measured. :Do not speak of them as if they are the same. ;(3) :The living things of the manifest world [顯界, ''kenkai''] are governed by Ōkuninushi [大國主, a prominent Shinto deity of the terrestrial realm]. :However, he cannot entirely protect their spirit, for it is a matter of the soul-body [魄體, ''hakutai'' - the physical or lower aspect of the soul]. ;(4) :What the Chinese call the "Mean" (''Zhongyong''), "Central Harmony" (''Zhonghe''), or the "Great Center" (''Daizhong'')—this "center" is vastly different from the "Center" of the divine way. :Do not view them as the same. ;(5) :The human heart [人心, ''jinshin''] is a branch of the Great Spirit [大精神, ''Dai-seishin'' - the ultimate, supreme God]. :Therefore, it has no birth and no death. :It is by this that one should be controlled. :However, in the present age, people take the Sun, the Earth, the Moon, and human souls to be their respective guardian deities. :These are all teachings that lack the doctrine of the Divine Office [神府, ''Shinfu'' - the true divine system]. :They should not be believed or followed. ;(6) :The Supreme God [上帝, ''Jōtei''] creates the heart from Four Souls and One Spirit [四魂一霊, ''shikon ichirei''] and bestows it upon living things. :The Master of the Earth [地主, ''Jinushi''] creates the body from Three Elements and Eight Powers [三元八力, ''sangen hachiriki''] and gives it to all of existence. :Therefore, that which protects one's spirit is the body, and that which protects one's body is the spirit. :There is no other god that guards them. :This is the command of the Divine Office, eternally unchanging. ;(7) :Even the greatest sages and wisest men acclaimed by the people are not, when seen through the eye of God, entirely perfect. :How much more so for all others? Therefore, it is certain that an ancestral spirit cannot protect a descendant's soul. ;(8) :The body of the Great Spirit is so vast that there is nothing outside it, and so minuscule that there is nothing inside it. :It is as if it exists nowhere, yet it is as if there is nowhere it does not exist. :The eye of a sage cannot perceive it; the mouth of a wise man cannot speak of it. :Therefore, the Imperial Canons [皇典, ''kōten''] state: "To be of hidden body is the definition of a god [神, ''kami'']." How wondrously spiritual and exquisitely mysterious it is! ;(9) :The Great Spirit hears what has no sound and sees what has no form. :Therefore, it knows the past and perceives the not-yet-happened. :The Great Spirit bestows this ultimate spirituality upon the Children of God [神子, ''shinko'' - humanity]. :The Children of God who revere and uphold this will achieve the highest virtue. :This is the foundation of governing the heart and the essential of cultivating the self. ;(10) :When a Child of God cultivates a good heart, the Great Spirit grants them a spiritual soul [靈魂, ''reikon'']. :When a Child of God disorders their good conduct, the Great Spirit seizes their soul-essence [靈魄, ''reihaku'']. :The speed of this granting and seizing follows as a shadow follows a form. :How can one not be in awe of the Great Spirit? ;(11) :The Great Spirit has no evil, and the original ancestor also had no evil. :That among the thousands of descendants there are occasionally those with evil and sin is entirely the result of their own actions and their own reaping. :It is not an inheritance from the original ancestor. :The Divine Canons [神典, ''shinten''] state: "To correct one's words is to correct one's faults," which means to be without evil. :Why do people absent-mindedly wait for what comes after death? ;(12) :Reflection, shame, remorse, awe, and awakening: these five emotions are contained within the soul. :They are the commandments of the divine intelligence. :In these latter days, the ignorant create arbitrary precepts to dazzle and confuse later students. :They can be called the criminal slaves of the Divine Office. ;(13) :To esteem the heart and humble the body is the foundation of goodness. :To value the body and cheapen the heart is the beginning of evil. :Therefore, it is said: "Goodness is the common property of the world; evil is the private possession of a single person." Those with a correct heart and virtuous conduct are good. :Those with an incorrect heart and no virtuous conduct are evil. :Though they are all Children of God, the distance between them is like that between the clouds and the mud. :Whose fault is this? ;(14) :The Wild Soul [荒魂, ''aramitama''] is divine valor. :The Harmonious Soul [和魂, ''nigimitama''] is divine affinity. :The Wondrous Soul [奇魂, ''kushimitama''] is divine wisdom. :The Fortunate Soul [幸魂, ''sachimitama''] is divine love. :These are what is called the spiritual soul, and the Upright Spirit [直靈, ''naohi''] is their master. :Vulgar scholarship does not recognize this, taking the Wild and Harmonious souls as the soul's substance, and the Wondrous and Fortunate souls as its function. :It does not know what the Upright Spirit is. :Is this not to be lamented? ;(15) :In esoteric rites [幽齋, ''yūsai''], one communicates spirit to Spirit. :In exoteric rites [顯齋, ''kensai''], one communicates form to form. :Therefore, esoteric rites have no divine images or shrines, and one prays to the true God. :Exoteric rites have divine images and palaces, and one worships the multitude of gods. :Vulgar scholarship is blind and ignorant of the ancient meaning, mixing them into one, or branching them into ten thousand, with no end to the confusion. :This is truly the great flaw in the meaning of worship. ;(16) :What is valued in one's life are fame, status, longevity, and wealth. :The one who grants and takes these away is the Great Spirit Soul [大靈魂, ''daireikon'']. :This is the proper desire bestowed by God. :Vulgar scholarship, not understanding this, is reckless and self-abandoning, seeking to find value in external things. :How could it possibly be attained? ;(17) :Everyone believes that judgment occurs after death and that reward and punishment do not happen in life. :Therefore, they take life lightly and death gravely. :This is the origin of incurring divine punishment and the cause of being bound in the celestial prison. :The eye of God is brilliant and makes no distinction between the unseen and the manifest worlds. :Life and death are a single principle. :Why make them into two? ;(18) :Heaven gives birth to people with one life and no second life. :This is the Mandate of Heaven. :However, in the world, there are often those who speak of the body's resurrection. :This is all fraud and falsehood. :When illuminated by the divine mirror of the sun, they will eventually incur divine wrath, their bodies will perish and their souls will be punished. :What hope have they then for being by the Emperor's [God's] side? Alas. ;(19) :The spirits of the ten thousand things are also bestowed by God. :However, the bodies they receive have distinctions of noble and humble, large and small. :Therefore, each has beauty and ugliness, wisdom and foolishness. :Their thousand changes and ten thousand transformations are as if without end. :How vast is the working of the Great Spirit! ;(20) :Spiritology [靈學, ''reigaku''] takes the purification of the heart as its foundation. :Therefore, the Imperial Deities [皇神, ''kōshin''] take the pacification of the soul [鎭魂, ''chinkon''] as their main principle. :The people revere and uphold this, daily training their true hearts. :The commentary on the ancient law code [令義解, ''Ryō no gige''] says: "'Pacification' means 'to soothe'; it speaks of summoning the wandering vital soul to remain in the central府 (''fu'', office/seat) of the body." One can see this was a vital rite of the nation. :People today are ignorant and stubbornly disregard it, seeking methods abroad and searching for arts in foreign lands. :This has become a habit, and their defilement increases daily. :Alas, how tragic this is! ;(21) :The ancients called the body ''inochi'' [命, life]. :They called the spirit ''kami'' [神, god/spirit]. :''Inochi'' refers to the distinctness and separateness of bodies. :''Kami'' refers to the meaning of an esoteric body or a hidden form. :Later students are careless and do not grasp the ancient meaning, using ''inochi'' and ''kami'' interchangeably and referring to the unseen and manifest worlds by the same name. :This can be called chaos. ;(22) :The small spirit [小精神, ''shō-seishin'' - the individual human spirit] is in constant motion and stillness, appearing and disappearing without end. :It is revealed in one's voice and takes form on one's skin. :A sage cannot hide his intentions; a lesser person cannot conceal their thoughts. :That the laws of God [神憲, ''shinken''] cannot be obscured is indeed so. ;(23) :The Divine Light is called ''Takamimusubi'' [高皇產靈, a primordial creator deity]. :The Divine Warmth is called ''Kamimusubi'' [神皇產靈, another primordial creator deity]. :Both are tied to the names of the original ancestors. ;(24) :The five arts of medicine, bathing, defending, discarding, and avoiding are certainly within the sentiments of the populace. :In later generations, people created different practices, basely chanting strange spells. :In their desire to purify the self, they instead defile the body. :This is truly a flaw in a perfect white jewel. ==Sources== *[https://wikisource.org/wiki/%E9%81%93%E4%B9%8B%E5%A4%A7%E5%8E%9F 道之大原] on Wikisource [[Category:Honda Chikaatsu]]

revision.comment: row breaks
revision.text:
'''''Michi no Taigen''''' (道之大原) was originally written in March 1883 by [[w:en:Honda Chikaatsu|Honda Chikaatsu]] ([[w:ja:本田親徳|本田親徳]]), a 19th-century Japanese writer, in Literary Chinese (hence the lack of Japanese ''[[w:en:Kana|kana]]'' in the text, although the text also has various Japanese idiosyncrasies). ''Michi no taigen'' (道之大原) can also be found in ''The essence of chinkon kishin'' ([[commons:Category:鎮魂帰神の極意|鎮魂帰神の極意]], ''Chinkon kishin no gokui''), which was published by [[w:en:Shintō Tenkōkyo|Shintō Tenkōkyo]] founder Tomokiyo Yoshisane ([[w:ja:友清歓真|友清歓真]]) in 1920. This version is based on Tomokiyo's 1920 edition. The text consists of 24 sections about the nature of the divine, the formation of the spirit and the soul, and so on. Some of the punctuation has been added from the ''Michi no taigen'' text in: Suzuki, Shigemichi 鈴木重道 (ed.). 1976. ''Honda Chikaatsu zenshū'' 本田親徳全集. Kawaguchi: Sangabō 山雅房.
Some corrections to Tomokiyo's (1920) ''Michi no taigen'' text have been included from: Satō, Akihiko 佐藤卿彦. 1978. ''Kenshin Honda reigaku hōten'' 顕神本田霊学法典. Kawaguchi: Sangabō 山雅房. These corrections are given in square brackets [ ]. ==Original text== ;道之大原註釋 :本田親德先生遺稿 :友清九語謹註 (1) :天[主]創造一物。 :悉因力德。 :故善惡相混 ::美醜互交。 (2) :萬物之中也者。 :有形之中也。 :其中可測矣。 :神界之中也者。 :無形之中也。 :其中不可測矣。 :勿混語。 (3) :顯界之活物者係 ::大國主之所轄。 :然而不能悉守其魂 ::乃魄體之故也。 (4) :漢人所謂中庸、中和、大中。 :其中者與神父之中逈別。 :勿同視。 (5) :人心也者大精神之分派。 :故無生無死。 :爲之所制御。 :而今時以太陽、大地、大陰及人魂。 :爲各位之守神。 :悉皆無神府之說。 :不可信從。 (6) :上帝以四魂一靈造心。 :而賦之活物。 :地主以三元八力造體。 :而與之萬有。 :故守其靈者其體。 :守其體者其靈。 :菲有他神守之也。 :是乃神府之命永遠不易。 (7) :至聖大賢 ::斯民所稱。 :神眼視之 ::未盡全美。 :况於其他乎。 :故先靈不能守後魂必矣。 (8) :大精神之爲體也。 :至大無外 ::至小無內。 :若無所在。 :若無不所在。 :聖眼不能視之。 :賢口不能語之。 :故皇典曰隱身也者 ::則神之義。 :宜哉其靈靈妙妙。 (9) :大精神也者。 :聽於無聲。 :視於無形。 :故知旣往 ::察未然。 :大精神以此至靈賚神子。 :神子尊奉 ::竟成至德。 :是治心之本。 :修身之要。 (10) :神子治善心。 :大精神與之靈魂。 :神子亂良行。 :大精神奪之靈魄。 :其與奪之速如影從形。 :豈可不畏哉大精神。 (11) :大精神無惡 ::而始祖亦無惡。 :裔孫千百中 ::偶有惡罪者 ::悉皆自業自得。 :非始祖之遺。 :神典曰改言乃改過 ::無惡之意。 :何碌碌待死後之爲。 (12) :省。耻。悔。畏。 :覺之五情含有靈魂中。 :乃神明之戒律也。 :末世無識妄作戒律眩惑後學。 :可謂神府之罪奴。 (13) :尊心卑體爲善之本。 :貴體賤心成惡之始。 :故曰善天下所公共。 :惡一人所私有。 :正心德行者善也。 :不正無行者惡也。 :均是神子。 :其相遠恰如雲埿。 :是夫誰過。 (14) :荒魂者神勇。 :和魂者神親。 :奇魂者神智。 :幸魂者神愛。 :乃所謂靈魂 ::而直靈者主宰之。 :俗學不識。 :以荒和爲心之體。 :以奇幸爲心之用。 :不知直靈爲何物。 :豈可不悲哉。 (15) :幽齋以靈對靈。 :顯齋以形對形。 :故幽齋無神像宮社 ::而祈眞神。 :顯齋有神像宮殿 ::而祭衆神。 :俗學蒙眛不知古義。 :混以爲一。 :岐以爲萬。 :無所底止。 :實祭義之大疵。 (16) :所貴于身者。 :名、位、壽、富。 :而與[奪]之者大靈魂也。 :是即神賦之正欲。 :俗學不悟自暴自棄。 :將求貴于外。 :何夫可得哉。 (17) :人皆以爲審判在死後 ::而賞罰不在生前。 :故輕于生 ::而重于死。 :是則蒙神誅之原。 :繫天獄之因[者]。 :神眼赫赫 ::固無幽顯。 :死生一理。 :何爲二之。 (18) :天降生民有一生 ::而無再生。 :是乃天命。 :然而世間往往有言體甦者。 :悉皆詐僞。 :日照神鏡竟觸神怒體斃魂誅。 :亦何望帝側噫。 (19) :萬物精神亦神之所賦與。 :雖然其所受[之]體 ::有尊、有卑、有大、有小。 :故各有美、有惡、有賢、有愚。 :其千變萬化 ::如無究盡。 :大哉大精神之用。 (20) :靈學者以淨心爲本。 :故皇神以鎭魂爲之主。 :百[姓]尊奉日練眞心。 :令義解云鎭者安也 ::言招離遊之運魂留身體之中府。 :可見其國家之重典。 :今人蒙眛頑乎不顧。 :求法于外。 :尋術于異。 :慣習爲常。 :汚穢日加。 :噫悲[矣]哉。 (21) :古稱體曰命。 :稱靈曰神。 :命也者體異體別也。 :神也者幽體隱身之義也。 :後學鹵莽不達古義。 :命神混用。 :幽顯同稱。 :可謂亂。 (22) :小精神 ::動靜無常 ::出沒無窮。 :顯其聲音。 :形其皮膚。 :聖賢不能秘其意。 :小人不能隱其想。 :乃神憲之不可掩者蓋如斯。 (23) :神光謂之高皇產靈。 :神温謂之神皇產靈。 :皆係于始祖之所名。 (24) :藥、浴、防、棄、避之五術 ::固在衆情中。 :後世動作異行 ::猥誦奇咒。 :欲潔其身 ::反汚其體。 :實白圭之玷。 ==English translation== (Google Gemini 2.5 Pro translation; please check for errors and improvements) '''Annotations on the Great Origin of the Way :''(Dō no Taigen Chūshaku)'' ;Posthumous Manuscripts of Master Honda Chikaatsu ;Respectfully Annotated by Tomokiyo Kyūgo ;(1) :When the Lord of Heaven [天主, ''Tenshu''] creates a single thing, it is done entirely through His power and virtue. :Therefore, good and evil are intermingled, and beauty and ugliness intersect. ;(2) :The "within" of the ten thousand things is a "within" that has form. :Its interior can be measured. :The "within" of the divine realm is a "within" that is formless. :Its interior cannot be measured. :Do not speak of them as if they are the same. ;(3) :The living things of the manifest world [顯界, ''kenkai''] are governed by Ōkuninushi [大國主, a prominent Shinto deity of the terrestrial realm]. :However, he cannot entirely protect their spirit, for it is a matter of the soul-body [魄體, ''hakutai'' - the physical or lower aspect of the soul]. ;(4) :What the Chinese call the "Mean" (''Zhongyong''), "Central Harmony" (''Zhonghe''), or the "Great Center" (''Daizhong'')—this "center" is vastly different from the "Center" of the divine way. :Do not view them as the same. ;(5) :The human heart [人心, ''jinshin''] is a branch of the Great Spirit [大精神, ''Dai-seishin'' - the ultimate, supreme God]. :Therefore, it has no birth and no death. :It is by this that one should be controlled. :However, in the present age, people take the Sun, the Earth, the Moon, and human souls to be their respective guardian deities. :These are all teachings that lack the doctrine of the Divine Office [神府, ''Shinfu'' - the true divine system]. :They should not be believed or followed. ;(6) :The Supreme God [上帝, ''Jōtei''] creates the heart from Four Souls and One Spirit [四魂一霊, ''shikon ichirei''] and bestows it upon living things. :The Master of the Earth [地主, ''Jinushi''] creates the body from Three Elements and Eight Powers [三元八力, ''sangen hachiriki''] and gives it to all of existence. :Therefore, that which protects one's spirit is the body, and that which protects one's body is the spirit. :There is no other god that guards them. :This is the command of the Divine Office, eternally unchanging. ;(7) :Even the greatest sages and wisest men acclaimed by the people are not, when seen through the eye of God, entirely perfect. :How much more so for all others? :Therefore, it is certain that an ancestral spirit cannot protect a descendant's soul. ;(8) :The body of the Great Spirit is so vast that there is nothing outside it, and so minuscule that there is nothing inside it. :It is as if it exists nowhere, yet it is as if there is nowhere it does not exist. :The eye of a sage cannot perceive it; the mouth of a wise man cannot speak of it. :Therefore, the Imperial Canons [皇典, ''kōten''] state: "To be of hidden body is the definition of a god [神, ''kami'']." How wondrously spiritual and exquisitely mysterious it is! ;(9) :The Great Spirit hears what has no sound and sees what has no form. :Therefore, it knows the past and perceives the not-yet-happened. :The Great Spirit bestows this ultimate spirituality upon the Children of God [神子, ''shinko'' - humanity]. :The Children of God who revere and uphold this will achieve the highest virtue. :This is the foundation of governing the heart and the essential of cultivating the self. ;(10) :When a Child of God cultivates a good heart, the Great Spirit grants them a spiritual soul [靈魂, ''reikon'']. :When a Child of God disorders their good conduct, the Great Spirit seizes their soul-essence [靈魄, ''reihaku'']. :The speed of this granting and seizing follows as a shadow follows a form. :How can one not be in awe of the Great Spirit? ;(11) :The Great Spirit has no evil, and the original ancestor also had no evil. :That among the thousands of descendants there are occasionally those with evil and sin is entirely the result of their own actions and their own reaping. :It is not an inheritance from the original ancestor. :The Divine Canons [神典, ''shinten''] state: "To correct one's words is to correct one's faults," which means to be without evil. :Why do people absent-mindedly wait for what comes after death? ;(12) :Reflection, shame, remorse, awe, and awakening: these five emotions are contained within the soul. :They are the commandments of the divine intelligence. :In these latter days, the ignorant create arbitrary precepts to dazzle and confuse later students. :They can be called the criminal slaves of the Divine Office. ;(13) :To esteem the heart and humble the body is the foundation of goodness. :To value the body and cheapen the heart is the beginning of evil. :Therefore, it is said: "Goodness is the common property of the world; evil is the private possession of a single person." Those with a correct heart and virtuous conduct are good. :Those with an incorrect heart and no virtuous conduct are evil. :Though they are all Children of God, the distance between them is like that between the clouds and the mud. :Whose fault is this? ;(14) :The Wild Soul [荒魂, ''aramitama''] is divine valor. :The Harmonious Soul [和魂, ''nigimitama''] is divine affinity. :The Wondrous Soul [奇魂, ''kushimitama''] is divine wisdom. :The Fortunate Soul [幸魂, ''sachimitama''] is divine love. :These are what is called the spiritual soul, and the Upright Spirit [直靈, ''naohi''] is their master. :Vulgar scholarship does not recognize this, taking the Wild and Harmonious souls as the soul's substance, and the Wondrous and Fortunate souls as its function. :It does not know what the Upright Spirit is. :Is this not to be lamented? ;(17) :Everyone believes that judgment occurs after death and that reward and punishment do not happen in life. :Therefore, they take life lightly and death gravely. :This is the origin of incurring divine punishment and the cause of being bound in the celestial prison. :The eye of God is brilliant and makes no distinction between the unseen and the manifest worlds. :Life and death are a single principle. :Why make them into two? ;(18) :Heaven gives birth to people with one life and no second life. :This is the Mandate of Heaven. :However, in the world, there are often those who speak of the body's resurrection. :This is all fraud and falsehood. :When illuminated by the divine mirror of the sun, they will eventually incur divine wrath, their bodies will perish and their souls will be punished. :What hope have they then for being by the Emperor's [God's] side? Alas. ;(19) :The spirits of the ten thousand things are also bestowed by God. :However, the bodies they receive have distinctions of noble and humble, large and small. :Therefore, each has beauty and ugliness, wisdom and foolishness. :Their thousand changes and ten thousand transformations are as if without end. :How vast is the working of the Great Spirit! ;(20) :Spiritology [靈學, ''reigaku''] takes the purification of the heart as its foundation. :Therefore, the Imperial Deities [皇神, ''kōshin''] take the pacification of the soul [鎭魂, ''chinkon''] as their main principle. :The people revere and uphold this, daily training their true hearts. :The commentary on the ancient law code [令義解, ''Ryō no gige''] says: "'Pacification' means 'to soothe'; it speaks of summoning the wandering vital soul to remain in the central府 (''fu'', office/seat) of the body." One can see this was a vital rite of the nation. :People today are ignorant and stubbornly disregard it, seeking methods abroad and searching for arts in foreign lands. :This has become a habit, and their defilement increases daily. :Alas, how tragic this is! ;(21) :The ancients called the body ''inochi'' [命, life]. :They called the spirit ''kami'' [神, god/spirit]. :''Inochi'' refers to the distinctness and separateness of bodies. :''Kami'' refers to the meaning of an esoteric body or a hidden form. :Later students are careless and do not grasp the ancient meaning, using ''inochi'' and ''kami'' interchangeably and referring to the unseen and manifest worlds by the same name. :This can be called chaos. ;(22) :The small spirit [小精神, ''shō-seishin'' - the individual human spirit] is in constant motion and stillness, appearing and disappearing without end. :It is revealed in one's voice and takes form on one's skin. :A sage cannot hide his intentions; a lesser person cannot conceal their thoughts. :That the laws of God [神憲, ''shinken''] cannot be obscured is indeed so. ;(23) :The Divine Light is called ''Takamimusubi'' [高皇產靈, a primordial creator deity]. :The Divine Warmth is called ''Kamimusubi'' [神皇產靈, another primordial creator deity]. :Both are tied to the names of the original ancestors. ;(24) :The five arts of medicine, bathing, defending, discarding, and avoiding are certainly within the sentiments of the populace. :In later generations, people created different practices, basely chanting strange spells. :In their desire to purify the self, they instead defile the body. :This is truly a flaw in a perfect white jewel. ==Sources== *[https://wikisource.org/wiki/%E9%81%93%E4%B9%8B%E5%A4%A7%E5%8E%9F 道之大原] on Wikisource [[Category:Honda Chikaatsu]]
